turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Thomas has our opinion in case 15-1039 Sandoz, Inc. versus Amgen, Inc. and the cross-petition.

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: These cases come to us on a writ of certiorari to United States Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit. The Biologics Price Competition and Innovation Act of 2009 governs a type of drug called biosimilar. A biosimilar is a biological product, that is a drug derived from natural sources that is highly similar to another biological product that has already been approved by the Food and Drug Administration. Under the Act, an applicant that seeks FDA approval of a biosimilar must provide its application materials and manufacturing information to the manufacturer of the corresponding biologic known as the sponsor within 20 days of the date on which the FDA notifies the applicant that it has accepted the application for review. Before marketing the biosimiliar commercially, the applicant must give the sponsor at least a 180 days’ notice. In these cases, Sandoz filed an application with the FDA for a biosimilar but declined to provide its application materials to Amgen, the sponsor of the corresponding biologic. Sandoz gave notice of the commercial marketing but did so before receiving a license from the FDA. Amgen sued seeking injunctions, compelling disclosure of the application materials and requiring Sandoz to provide notice after obtaining a license from FDA. The questions presented are whether Amgen may obtain such relief. We hold that the act’s requirement that an applicant provide the sponsor with its application materials and manufacturing information is not enforceable by an injunction under federal law. The act does not expressly provide for an injunction. Instead it provides a different remedy for an applicant’s failure to disclose the requisite information that is it authorizes the sponsor, but not the applicant to bring an immediate declaratory judgment action for infringement. The presence of this remedy coupled with the absence of any other textually specified remedies, indicates that Congress did not intend that the sponsors or for the sponsors to have access to injunctive relief at least as a matter of federal law. Amgen also sought relief under California’s unfair competition statue which provides a remedy for unlawful conduct. Whether Sandoz’s failure to disclose its application was unlawful within the meaning of this statue is a question of state law and in our view, the Court of Appeals erred in attempting to answer that question by referring only to federal law. We also hold that the applicant may give notice of commercial marketing to the sponsor either before or after obtaining a license from the FDA for the biosimilar. The act states that the applicant shall provide notice to the referenced product sponsor not later than 180 days before the date of the first commercial marketing of the biosimilar licensed under the act. The most natural reading of this language is that the biosimilar must be licensed at the time of commercial marketing, not at the time of notice. For these and other reasons contained in our opinion, the judgment of the federal circuit is vacated in part and reversed in part, and the cases are remanded. The decision is unanimous. Justice Breyer has filed a concurring opinion.
